description: Think selling stocks in the red is a complete failure? In this comprehensive guide, discover how elite investors utilize this specific strategy to convert their losing trades into guaranteed IRS savings.

schemas.headline: Tax Loss Harvesting Explained: Turn Losing Stocks Into Tax Savings
schemas.description: Think selling stocks in the red is a complete failure? In this comprehensive guide, discover how elite investors utilize this specific strategy to convert their losing trades into guaranteed IRS savings.

acceptedAnswer.text: Tax loss harvesting is an investment strategy where you sell securities at a loss to offset a capital gains tax liability. It allows you to reduce your overall tax bill while maintaining your market position by buying a similar, but not substantially identical, asset.

mainEntity.name: How does tax loss harvesting work?

acceptedAnswer.text: It works by selling a stock or ETF that has dropped in value. The realized loss is then used to offset any realized capital gains from other investments. If your losses exceed your gains, you can offset up to $3,000 of ordinary income per year, and carry forward any remaining losses to future years.

mainEntity.name: What are the key tax loss harvesting rules?

acceptedAnswer.text: The most important rules include the wash sale rule, which prevents you from claiming the loss if you buy a 'substantially identical' asset within 30 days before or after the sale. Also, short-term losses first offset short-term gains, and long-term losses first offset long-term gains.

mainEntity.name: How much can you deduct with tax loss harvesting?

acceptedAnswer.text: If your capital losses exceed your capital gains, you can deduct up to $3,000 of those losses against your ordinary income in a given tax year. Any losses beyond that $3,000 limit can be carried forward indefinitely into future tax years.

mainEntity.name: Is tax loss harvesting worth it?

acceptedAnswer.text: It is generally worth it if you have highly taxed short-term capital gains, are in a high income tax bracket, or have significant portfolio losses. It may not make sense if you are in a very low tax bracket where capital gains taxes are already 0%, or if transaction costs outweigh the tax benefits.
